caveats: a: BLS data are for NAICS 311900 and include other food manufacturing activities.
a: Vendor capabilities demonstrate feasibility, not current penetration or net labor removal.
a: The range excludes tasks already automated at the current baseline.

rho: Vendor materials may emphasize successful use cases.
rho: Implementation depends on plant layout, throughput, product mix, and existing controls.
e: Public examples establish that eligible models exist but do not measure their prevalence.
e: Many firms combine branded and outsourced revenue, making eligibility sensitive to the revenue-share threshold.
e: The frozen LMM firm-count estimate may include establishments or firms with economics unlike the screened population.

s5: Survey intentions are not completed transactions.
s5: Manufacturing-sector ownership statistics include much larger and structurally different businesses.

q: Retention differs between private-label tenders, contract manufacturing, foodservice, and customized ingredient programs.
q: This estimate does not assume demand growth or implementation success, which belong in other primitives.
d5: Peanut utilization is not the entire roasted-nut and nut-butter service basket.
d5: USDA data include branded and vertically integrated production outside the lens.
d5: Constant-price demand can diverge from reported shipment value because nut commodity prices are volatile.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Roasted Nuts and Peanut Butter Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B production runs and supply programs for retailers, food brands, distributors, restaurants, institutions, and ingredient users, commonly priced through bids, product specifications, purchase orders, and fixed-price commitments rather than time-and-materials billing.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to private-label, contract-manufacturing, foodservice, institutional, and commercial-ingredient suppliers because NAICS 311911 also contains branded-only consumer manufacturers whose economics are driven by brand ownership rather than an outsourced service relationship.
lens.excluded_activities: Branded-only consumer-product manufacturers without recurring outsourced customer work; captive plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles; candy-coated nut manufacturing classified elsewhere; and oilseed-butter manufacturing classified outside NAICS 311911.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. manufacturers in NAICS 311911 that repeatedly roast, season, process, blend, or package nuts and nut butters for external customers under private-label, contract-manufacturing, foodservice, institutional, or commercial-ingredient arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation employment and wages for broader NAICS 311900, not task-level wage exposure for 311911. I map planning, clerical, inspection, packaging-control, and selected production-support work to AI-addressable tasks, while excluding direct physical handling and already-automated inspection. The broader category may overstate office work, while its employment rather than establishment-specific task data may understate mixed-role automation in small plants.
caveats: BLS data are for NAICS 311900 and include other food manufacturing activities.
Vendor capabilities demonstrate feasibility, not current penetration or net labor removal.
The range excludes tasks already automated at the current baseline.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll-weighted task inventories from 10-20 LMM nut processors, including current sorter/vision-system penetration, manual inspection hours, packaging-line staffing, production-planning effort, and back-office headcount by role.
a.rationale: The broader industry is production- and material-handling-heavy, limiting the addressable wage pool, but it also has meaningful quality inspection, packaging, production-control, inventory, and administrative work. Commercial AI vision systems already automate nut-defect detection and quality monitoring, supporting additional exposure in inspection and line-control workflows beyond office automation.

d5.bridge: USDA measures national peanut-food consumption categories and forecasts rather than constant-price demand for the narrowed outsourced-service basket across all nuts. I use the long-run strength in peanut butter, softness in peanut snacks, and current forecast decline to anchor near-flat year-five demand. This may understate growth in premium tree-nut products but overstate firms exposed to mature or retailer-consolidated programs.
caveats: Peanut utilization is not the entire roasted-nut and nut-butter service basket.
USDA data include branded and vertically integrated production outside the lens.
Constant-price demand can diverge from reported shipment value because nut commodity prices are volatile.
d5.change_evidence: Acquire five-year volume histories and customer forecasts by product and channel for LMM processors, plus Circana/Nielsen retail unit data and USDA category forecasts covering tree nuts as well as peanuts.
d5.rationale: Peanut butter, the largest U.S. food use for peanuts, remained above its prior ten-year average in 2024/25, supporting durability; the February 2026 outlook then forecast peanut food use down 1.6% and peanut-butter consumption down 4% from both the prior year and three-year average. Mixed recent evidence argues for essentially flat real, constant-quality demand rather than strong secular growth or collapse.

caveats: Public examples establish that eligible models exist but do not measure their prevalence.
Many firms combine branded and outsourced revenue, making eligibility sensitive to the revenue-share threshold.
The frozen LMM firm-count estimate may include establishments or firms with economics unlike the screened population.
e.change_evidence: Build an establishment-level list from Data Axle, PitchBook, FDA registrations, customer websites, and SIC/NAICS directories, then code each LMM firm's revenue mix for private label, contract manufacturing, foodservice, institutional, and commercial ingredients.
e.rationale: Industry examples confirm that private-label peanut butter, contract processing and packaging, foodservice, commercial ingredients, and customer-specification manufacturing are established channels. The code also contains branded manufacturers, so not every LMM firm meets the outsourced-service lens; absent a firm-level channel census, a modest majority is the bounded base judgment.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311911 ($1,088,606,391) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311911 ($13,959,345,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $739,882 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311911); class <5: 59 firms, avg revenue/firm $914,424 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $139,450 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,998,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $304,809 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,830,324 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $736,624 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 59 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,461,102 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,272,818 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $135,537,781 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $20,669,512 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $404,488,950 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,684,565 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 59

o.bridge: The NAICS definition, operating-company descriptions, and FDA requirements establish that the service is physical food transformation and regulated production, not a software-only output. I translate that operating model into the share of year-five demand still requiring an accountable outsourced manufacturer. This could overstate operator need if major customers insource, consolidate suppliers, or shift to minimally processed substitutes.
caveats: Operator requirement does not ensure that the same LMM suppliers retain the volume.
Customer insourcing and supplier consolidation could shift demand away from the lens without eliminating consumption.
The estimate assumes no discontinuous substitute for physical nut processing.
o.change_evidence: Interview major retail, brand, foodservice, and ingredient customers about insourcing plans, supplier consolidation, alternative products, and required third-party certifications; compare five-year make-versus-buy decisions and plant investments.
o.rationale: Roasting, grinding, seasoning, allergen control, validated kill steps, packaging, quality assurance, and food-safety records require a physical plant and accountable operator. Software and AI can change how the work is performed but cannot eliminate the underlying production service; limited loss is reserved for customer insourcing or channel substitution.

q.bridge: A public nut processor's filing describes periodic private-label bidding, fixed-price commitments, customer resistance to price increases, concentrated accounts, competitive pricing pressure, and a 72% material-cost share. I translate those commercial mechanisms into the discounted share of an implemented gross benefit retained by LMM outsourced manufacturers. The public company's scale and customer bargaining dynamics may understate retention for differentiated small-batch specialists but overstate it where LMM firms lack procurement leverage.
caveats: One public company's commercial structure may not represent smaller specialist processors.
Retention differs between private-label tenders, contract manufacturing, foodservice, and customized ingredient programs.
This estimate does not assume demand growth or implementation success, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: Review customer contracts, bid histories, renewal intervals, open-book or cost-plus clauses, price waterfalls, and post-productivity repricing across LMM private-label and contract-manufacturing accounts.
q.rationale: Operators should retain some automation benefit between repricing events and through improved quality, yield, and service, but transparent bids and renewals allow powerful retailers and brand customers to share savings. High material content also makes labor savings a relatively small part of delivered product cost, while customer concentration and competitive pricing cap durable retention.

rho.bridge: Commercial optical-sorter capabilities and factory-scale aflatoxin sorting show implementation feasibility for specific inspection tasks, while FDA controls describe compliance constraints across food plants. I translate these task and regulatory observations into the five-year implemented share of the broader exposed opportunity. Vendor evidence likely biases feasibility upward; validation, integration, capital, and change-management burdens bias realized adoption downward.
caveats: The aflatoxin study addresses one sorting application and one peanut type rather than the full task basket.
Vendor materials may emphasize successful use cases.
Implementation depends on plant layout, throughput, product mix, and existing controls.
rho.change_evidence: Collect five-year automation road maps, quoted installation costs, payback thresholds, validation timelines, false-reject rates, downtime, and post-install labor changes from LMM processors and equipment integrators.
rho.rationale: AI-enabled sorting and continuous quality monitoring are commercially deployable on nut-processing lines, and factory-scale research reports useful sensitivity without compromising throughput. Implementation should nevertheless be partial because brownfield integration, allergen controls, food-safety validation, recordkeeping, uptime, and thin technical teams constrain deployment, especially in LMM plants.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures intended sale or transfer among U.S. employer-business owners, and McKinsey summarizes owner age and projected exits for the broader manufacturing sector. I convert those cross-industry intentions and age distributions into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability for eligible 311911 LMM firms, discounting for intentions that do not close, family/internal transfers, and operations that are not transferable. Manufacturing's older owner base may make the all-employer Gallup rate conservative, while capital intensity and buyer constraints may make stated intentions optimistic.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year control-transfer incidence for NAICS 311911.
Survey intentions are not completed transactions.
Manufacturing-sector ownership statistics include much larger and structurally different businesses.
s5.change_evidence: Obtain owner-age and succession-plan data for a verified 311911 LMM firm list, plus five years of closed control transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, GF Data, and industry-focused brokers.
s5.rationale: Owner aging creates a credible supply of potential transfers, but a qualifying control transaction is materially narrower than an owner's intention to sell or transfer. Specialized plants, working capital, food-safety systems, customer concentration, and family succession can reduce completed third-party control transfers.

principal_driver: Durable physical outsourced production combined with commercially proven AI inspection and quality-monitoring tools.
principal_weakness: A material-heavy, competitively bid business gives large customers multiple paths to capture productivity gains at renewal.
